data.title: Meta wins halt to promotion of 'Careless People' tell-all book by former employee
data.description:
Meta Platforms on Wednesday won an emergency arbitration ruling to temporarily stop promotion of the tell-all book "Careless People" by a former employee, according to a copy of the ruling published by the social media company.

The book by Meta's former director of global public policy, Sarah Wynn-Williams, was called by the New York Times book review "an ugly, detailed portrait of one of the most powerful companies in the world," and its leading executives, including CEO Mark Zuckerberg, former Chief Operating Officer Sheryl Sandberg and Chief Global Affairs Officer Joel Kaplan.

Meta will suffer "immediate and irreparable loss" in the absence of an emergency relief, the American Arbitration Association's emergency arbitrator, Nicholas Gowen, said in a ruling after a hearing, which Wynn-Williams did not attend.

Book publisher Macmillan attended and argued it was not bound by the arbitration agreement, which was part of a severance agreement between the employee and company.

The ruling says that Wynn-Williams should stop promoting the book and, to the extent she could, stop further publication. It did not order any action by the publisher.

Meta spokesman Andy Stone said in a post on Threads: "This ruling affirms that Sarah Wynn Williams' false and defamatory book should never have been published.".

Wynn-Williams and Macmillan did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment on the ruling.

data.title: Mitsui Buys 50% of Hyperscale Data Center for 18 Billion Yen
data.description:
Mitsui & Co. will invest 18 billion yen to acquire 50% of a hyperscale data center in Kanagawa Prefecture through its subsidiary, Mitsui & Co. Asset Management Holdings, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The holding company managing the asset will secure non-recourse loans from financial institutions.

The operational data center has a power capacity of about 20 MW and will be included in a new data center fund Mitsui plans to launch with institutional investors.

Mitsui Realty Management will oversee asset management.

data.title: Nine Entertainment Appoints Stanton as Permanent CEO — Update
data.description:
By Stuart Condie

SYDNEY--Nine Entertainment appointed Matthew Stanton as chief executive following six months as interim boss, giving him a decision to make on the future of the Australian media conglomerate's real-estate venture.

Nine on Thursday said that Stanton had already reset the company's operating model since taking over from Mike Sneesby in September. Stanton, who will also be Nine's managing director, was previously chief finance and strategy officer.

Stanton will have to decide whether Nine holds onto its 60% stake in Domain, Australia's second-largest property advertiser. Last month, U.S. real-estate advertiser and analytics provider CoStar proposed a US$1.68 billion takeover of Domain.

Domain was spun out of Australian publisher Fairfax in 2017 and listed on the Australian Securities Exchange. Fairfax later merged with Nine, which responded to CoStar's proposal by saying that Domain was of strategic importance to its media ecosystem and long-term growth strategy.

Domain has long labored unsuccessfully to overcome REA Group's first-mover advantage in Australian online property classifieds, but still contributed 16% of Nine's revenue and 29% of its earnings over the six months through December.

Nine said that the appointment of Stanton, who has led transformation programs elsewhere including at Australian grocery giant Woolworths, followed a comprehensive global search.

"Matt's mix of strategic and commercial acumen, transformation and media experience, strong values, and an open and collaborative approach to leadership make him the right person," Nine Chair Catherine West said.

REA is controlled by News Corp, which also owns Dow Jones & Co., publisher of this newswire and The Wall Street Journal.

Write to Stuart Condie at stuart.condie@wsj.com

data.title: Nikkei Up 1.1%, Led by Electronics, Financial Stocks — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks are higher as concerns about U.S. inflation ebb for now. Electronics and financial stocks are leading the gains. Advantest is up 5.3% and Rakuten Bank is 4.5% higher. USD/JPY is at 148.35, compared with 148.07 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments in Japanese monetary policy ahead of the Bank of Japan's policy meeting next week. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 1.1% at 37232.93. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: UK House Price Balance Falls More Than Expected
data.description:
The UK Residential Market Survey by RICS revealed that the house price balance—tracking the difference between respondents reporting price increases and those noting declines—posted at +11% in February 2025, falling for the second straight months after a reading of +25% and +21% in December and January respectively.

The latest figure also came in way below forecasts of +20%.

Despite a modest rise in house prices, the pace of growth appears to have cooled across most regions.

However, Northern Ireland, Scotland, and the North West of England remain notable exceptions, with price indicators holding firm in expansionary territory.

Looking ahead, market uncertainty may weigh on house prices in the short term.

Nevertheless, the year-ahead price expectations balance stands at +47%, suggesting continued optimism about future growth.

data.title: Australian coal miners ease on lower Asia imports, soft prices
data.description:
** Australian coal miners fall as Asia's seaborne imports of metallurgical coal drop to the lowest in three years in February on lower demand from major fossil fuel consumers China and India

** The Newcastle coal index, a commonly used benchmark, fell 2.5% on March 10

** Whitehaven coal fall as much as 4.3%, which is its biggest percentage drop since February 18

** Coal-focused Coronado Global Resources slide 2.3%

** The slump comes despite weakening of fossil fuel regulations in the U.S. and increased pushback on net zero goals around the world

data.title: Metaplanet Adds 162 Bitcoin, Total Holdings at 3,050 BTC
data.description:
Metaplanet acquired 162 Bitcoin at an average price of 12.4 million yen each, totaling 2.01 billion yen, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

Total holdings rose to 3,050 BTC, with an average purchase price of 12.6 million yen, amounting to 38.5 billion yen.

BTC Yield for Q1 2025 was 53.2%, generating BTC Gain of 937.92 BTC and BTC (Yen) Gain of 11.05 billion yen.

Metaplanet raised 4 billion yen in zero-coupon bonds from EVO FUND in February, using proceeds for Bitcoin purchases. The company also issued 21 million stock acquisition rights to EVO FUND, with 1.2 million shares issued from the 13th tranche at an average price of 6,131 yen.

data.title: Intel's stock soars as Lip-Bu Tan is named CEO. But he faces a tough task ahead.
data.description:
By Emily Bary and Therese Poletti

With a permanent CEO, Intel now has some stability. But turning around the design and manufacturing businesses won't be easy.

Months after leaving Intel Corp.'s board of directors, Lip-Bu Tan is returning to the struggling chip giant - in a much bigger capacity.

He was named Intel's new chief executive on Wednesday afternoon, set to replace interim co-CEOs David Zinsner and Michelle (MJ) Johnston Holthaus, who have been holding down the top post since December. When he officially assumes his role on March 18, Tan will be tasked with restoring Intel to stability - and trying to get the company back to its former glory as a chip powerhouse.

Investors seem to like the move so far. Intel's stock (INTC) was surging more than 10% in the extended session Wednesday following the announcement.

Within a few minutes of the news, Intel shared an internal email that Tan wrote to employees after his appointment. Keeping Intel's core strength as an engineering company is a big part of Tan's vision, but the bigger question investors are asking is whether he wants to split Intel into two parts or keep it as one, with a separate manufacturing subsidiary.

"Under my leadership, Intel will be an engineering-focused company," he said. He did not give any clues his plans for the manufacturing business. "We will work hard to restore Intel's position as a world-class products company, establish ourselves as a world-class foundry," Tan said.

Tan takes over during a tumultuous time for Intel, which has struggled to keep pace with its semiconductor technology while it plows ahead with a costly bid to revive its foundry business. The company reportedly has been in talks with various suitors or partners who could get involved in various areas of its business.

Interestingly, Tan left Intel's board in August, amid reports that he did not agree with former CEO Pat Gelsinger's plan to keep Intel together as one company, with the manufacturing business as a separate subsidiary but still part of the chip giant.

"The split will come back into the headlines... because he was associated with having a strong appetite for the split, at least that was the belief," said Daniel Newman, CEO of the Futurum Group, told MarketWatch on Wednesday. "But he never publicly disagreed with the idea of one Intel at the time."

Tan previously led Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), which makes software that helps customers design chips, for a decade-plus stretch that ended in 2021. There, he oversaw "a reinvention of the company and drove a cultural transformation centered on customer-centric innovation," according to Intel's release. Cadence's stock rose more than 3,200% during his stint as CEO. He also is a founding managing partner of Walden Catalyst Ventures, a venture capital firm.

Intel's stock, meanwhile, has faced declines recently, falling about 50% in the past year. While the stock has seen some positive momentum year to date, much of that has been related to hopes for a takeover or other outside assistance, such as a joint venture involving Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSM) that could operate Intel's factories. Reuters reported on that prospect earlier Wednesday.

But it remains to be seen whether such an arrangement will come to be, partly since Intel and TSMC use different technologies. All the while, the manufacturing business continues to rack up losses for Intel, and the company isn't yet a meaningful player in AI semiconductors.

"The TSMC thing doesn't make a lot of sense to me," said Stacy Rasgon, a Bernstein Research analyst. "I just did a dinner with the head of Intel's foundry business and we were like 'What would be involved?' and it sounded like a non-starter to me. What he did say was that TSMC would bring a more customer-service mindset."

Newman agreed that it was possible that Tan may keep the company together. "I do think there is a stronger case... to keep it as one Intel," he added. "But Lip-Bu Tan would have to create a strong separation between church and state," he said, referring to potential customer fears that Intel's products business could gain access to rival's product designs being manufactured by its foundry subsidiary.

Tan could also embark on more cost-cutting measures at the chip giant, a la Hock Tan, the CEO of Broadcom (AVGO).

"He is going to be more of an efficiency driver, more focused, and you will see some headcount reduction," Newman predicted, adding that he expects a more lean and mean Intel. "Some of the same things Pat did, but Lip-Bu will be more aggressive on driving the roadmap and making sure that opex [operating expenses] is managed."

-Emily Bary -Therese Poletti

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Australia's MTM Critical Metals rises on MOU with India's Vedanta
data.description:
** Shares of miner MTM Critical Metals rise 6.7% to A$0.16

** Stock marks its biggest intraday gain since February 12

** Co signs MoU with Indian aluminium firm Vedanta for Red Mud (RM) recycling via its Flash Joule Heating (FJN)technology

** Tests at MTM's Houston facility reveal FJH efficiently converts RM's iron oxides into saleable compounds - MTM

** Adds that the process also improves iron-to-alumina ratio for cement additive use and reduces environmental risk by neutralising RM's alkalinity

** MTM shares down 42.3% YTD, VDAN shares nearly flat with minor gains of 0.1% YTD, as of last trade

data.title: Kudan Partners with Global Firm on AI Digital Twin Solutions
data.description:
Kudan said it partnered with a global industrial services provider to develop AI-powered digital twin solutions, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The deal combines Kudan's AI technology with the partner's industrial expertise to improve asset visualization, data accuracy, and process automation in manufacturing, logistics, and energy industries.

Pilot projects have shown improved asset recording accuracy and reduced manual input through AI-driven automation. Financial details were not disclosed.

data.title: Sunnova Prepares Restructuring Talks That Could Include Bankruptcy Filing — WSJ
data.description:
By Jodi Xu Klein and Alexander Gladstone

Sunnova Energy, one of the country's largest residential solar companies, is preparing to engage with creditors to explore options including a potential bankruptcy filing over its roughly $8.5 billion in debt, according to people familiar with the matter.

The company, working with law firm Baker Botts and investment bank JP Morgan, is putting together financial information and could begin negotiating with lenders and bondholders about ways to cut its debt and address near-term maturities, either in or out of bankruptcy court, said the people.

A group of holders of Sunnova's 2026 bonds and convertible notes might propose cutting the company's debtload by swapping some near-term bonds into equity, potentially offering some equity to junior convertible holders, the people said. The group is working with law firm Akin Gump Strauss Hauer & Feld and financial adviser Evercore, The Wall Street Journal previously reported.

Meanwhile, investment bank Ducera and law firm Paul Weiss are putting together a smaller group of debtholders, which is considering a proposal that could wipe out the convertible noteholders as well as equity, the people said.

Houston-based Sunnova, founded in 2012, is one of the main rooftop solar panel installers and financiers in the United States. Like most players in the sector, Sunnova has struggled as demand weakened for rooftop solar panel installations. Competitor SunPower filed for bankruptcy last year as its financial situation deteriorated.

Uncertainties around state and federal tax benefits for solar installations and the potential loss of future loan guarantees offered by the U.S. Department of Energy also are weighing on Sunnova's business outlook.

In 2023, the Energy Department, through the Loan Programs Office, committed to guaranteeing up to $3 billion in debt securities issued by Sunnova to support rooftop solar installations as part of the Biden administration's green financing initiative.

That initiative includes hundreds of billions of dollars in tax credits and loans for clean energy, climate tech startups and other green businesses. However, its future is uncertain as the Trump administration undertakes a sweeping overhaul of federal agencies.

Sunnova's most urgent task is addressing nearly $1 billion of bonds and convertible notes maturing in 2026, sources said.

Last year, the company worked with investment bank Moelis and law firm Kirkland & Ellis to kick-start debt negotiations with creditors holding the debt, the people said. Sunnova later suspended the talks and hired JPMorgan to raise financing.

Earlier this month, Sunnova announced KKR agreed to provide a $185 million loan backed by cash flows from asset-backed securities to boost the company's liquidity. The loan won't be included in a bankruptcy proceeding because it is secured by a separate set of assets, the people said.

Despite the loan, former Sunnova chief executive John Berger said earlier this month the company may not generate enough cash to remain operational due to weakness in the residential solar market. Sunnova reported a net loss of $448 million for 2024, according to its filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Berger, Sunnova's founder, stepped down as CEO this week. He was replaced by Paul Mathews, the company's chief operating officer.

Shares of Sunnova fell and closed at 36 cents on Wednesday. The stock is down 97% over the past six months.

Write to Jodi Xu Klein at jodi.klein@wsj.com and Alexander Gladstone at alexander.gladstone@wsj.com

data.title: New Zealand Market Less Optimistic Contact-Manawa Deal Gets Approval — Market Talk
data.description: New Zealand investors have dropped the chance of Contact Energy securing regulatory clearance for its takeover of Manawa Energy to less than 40%, Forsyth Barr says. It believes Contact has offered a full price for Manawa, and it will take several years to deliver savings needed to justify the transaction cost. Analyst Andrew Harvey-Green doesn't expect Contact's share price to react much whatever New Zealand's Commerce Commission decides about the deal. "The situation is different for Manawa," Forsyth Barr says. "The passage of time means it is increasingly difficult to estimate the size of the market reaction to no deal." It thinks Manawa's share price could fall to around NZ$4.00 if the takeover is blocked, from NZ$4.88 currently. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2004 ET - New Zealand investors have dropped the chance of Contact Energy securing regulatory clearance for its takeover of Manawa Energy to less than 40%, Forsyth Barr says. It believes Contact has offered a full price for Manawa, and it will take several years to deliver savings needed to justify the transaction cost. Analyst Andrew Harvey-Green doesn't expect Contact's share price to react much whatever New Zealand's Commerce Commission decides about the deal. "The situation is different for Manawa," Forsyth Barr says. "The passage of time means it is increasingly difficult to estimate the size of the market reaction to no deal." It thinks Manawa's share price could fall to around NZ$4.00 if the takeover is blocked, from NZ$4.88 currently. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1951 ET - Japanese stocks may rise as concerns about U.S. inflation recede for now. Nikkei futures are up 0.9% at 37135 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 148.25, compared with 148.07 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Besides U.S. trade policy, investors are focusing on any developments in Japanese monetary policy ahead of the Bank of Japan's policy meeting next week. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.1% to 36819.09 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1757 ET - Bank of Canada senior officials did discuss leaving its policy rate unchanged but opted for a quarter-point cut because of polling it conducted suggesting business and consumer confidence had turned sour due to tariff threats, Gov. Tiff Macklem tells Reuters. He says one scenario contemplated was keeping the policy rate at 3%. "The argument for staying at 3% was growth has been stronger than we expected," Macklem tells Reuters. During his press conference, Macklem said there was no serious thought given to a half-point cut. The fact some senior policymakers talked about leaving rates unchanged might help explain BOC's communications — which was relatively strident, or hawkish, about tariff-fueled inflation risks. The decision has traders and economists uncertain about an another cut in April.(paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1609 ET - Mexico's industrial production likely picked up in January after a decline in December, while remaining below year-earlier levels for a sixth consecutive month amid weakness in mining and construction. Industrial production is expected to have risen 0.4% seasonally adjusted from December, according to a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts. Production is expected to be down 1.7% unadjusted from January of 2024. Statistics institute Inegi is scheduled to report industrial production on Thursday. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1541 ET - Treasury yields rise as inflation cools but remain above the Fed's target, amid concerns that tariffs could keep prices hot in coming months. February PPI wholesale price index is due tomorrow. The EPA announces measures to reduce red tape, promising they will unleash investment and reduce energy and other costs. A 10-year Treasury auction is met with solid demand, with a bid-to-cover ratio of 2.59, a little above the 12-month average. Jobless claims tomorrow are expected to rise slightly, in a WSJ survey. The 10-year gains 0.029 percentage point to 4.315% and the two-year rises 0.055 p.p. to 3.994%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1502 ET - The recent turbulence in financial markets is making corporate bonds look cheaper relative to Treasurys, but still not enough to compensate for the increased risk stemming from policy uncertainty tied to President Trump, Madison Investments' Mike Sanders says. The ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread has widened to 3.2% as of yesterday, from 2.6% in late January, according to FRED data. "We have been reducing some relative credit risk in our portfolios," Sanders says. He is buying more mortgages and owns "a good amount" of Treasurys. "We are just kind of watching the market and seeing if there is an opportunity to add either mortgages or credit." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1434 ET - Royal Bank of Canada reckons there won't be a race to the bottom for Canadian interest rates beyond what it has already expected this year projecting the central bank's policy rate will decline to 2.25% around midyear. That's despite the turmoil the trade war with the U.S. is expected to bring. Economist Claire Fan says that after the latest quarter-point cut to 2.75% data releases will going forward be monitored closed for signs weaker sentiment is reflected in actual spending, GDP and labor numbers. Data also will be watched for any evidence the uptick in inflation in recent months is persisting and pushing up expectations, and for any fiscal response from Ottawa to addressing trade disruptions. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1423 ET - The team at TD Securities says further rate cuts from the Bank of Canada will require "tangible signs of economic weakness" from the U.S.-Canada trade conflict. The firm predicts that scenario will unfold, based on some of the results from early polling the Bank of Canada conducted, showing trepidation among households and business leaders about spending plans. The BOC "is telegraphing a gradual approach to future policy decisions, but we do not see inflation expectations as a material hurdle to another quarter-point cut in April," TD says. "Our view is that the downward pressures on economic activity will ultimately dominate." (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1419 ET - The bias in the Bank of Canada's comments around opting for a quarter-point rate cut is to the hawkish side of neutral as expected, says Scotiabank's Derek Holt. With its decision the central bank offered survey-based data to help assess the effects of tariffs, key to which are price signals, Holt says. The surveys find that about half of all businesses expect to raise prices if tariffs are in place and almost all of that half expect to pass through basically all of the higher tariffs into end prices. And consumer surveys show 72% expect higher prices due to tariffs and trade tensions. Holt notes markets have now scaled back bets for further BoC cuts. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1409 ET - Canada's government is imposing a 25% tariff on about C$30 billion of imports from the U.S., adding to the 25% levy on C$30 billion of imports already implemented. That leaves C$95 billion of postponed tariffs on American imports being targeted by Ottawa in response to the Trump's administration's tariffs. The effect will be to modestly add to imported price pressures, says Scotiabank's Derek Holt, noting Canada's move appears to be coordinate with the European Union's retaliation against Trump's steel and aluminum tariffs. "The overnight European retaliatory moves and now Canada's make it clear to the U.S. administration that its trade partners will not merely roll over in response to abusive U.S. trade policies," Holt says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1407 ET - The Bank of Canada has effectively confirmed it is hesitant to commit to much more in the way of policy support, putting at risk Capital Economics' forecast for further cuts at each of the next three policy meetings. The central bank cut by a quarter point and warned monetary policy can't offset the impacts of a trade war, with a need to guard against tariff-related price inflation. Capital's Stephen Brown notes that price changes tend to follow changes in demand with a lag, whereas tariff-related price hikes will be more immediate. That raises the risk the BoC will be slower to lower rates though for now he still expects quarter-point cuts until the policy rate reaches 2%.(robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1404 ET - BMO Capital Markets chief economist Doug Porter believes there are three more rate cuts coming from the Bank of Canada, regardless of the measured tone from Governor Tiff Macklem and the emphasis on tariff-related inflation risks. That would bring the BOC policy rate to 2%. Porter says BMO's call is based on the belief that Canada "will be facing some serious tariffs for an extended period of time and that the growth dampening aspects of the trade war will ultimately outweigh the upside inflationary impact." (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: Artisan says Seven & I independent director resignations show board dysfunction
data.description:
Artisan Partners, a U.S.-based investor in Japan's Seven & i , reiterated its criticism of governance at the retail giant after two independent directors resigned on Wednesday, having opposed the appointment of a new CEO earlier in the week.

"The recent resignations of two of the company's independent directors are a sign of dysfunction and will serve to further compromise the board function," Artisan said in a letter.

It follows a separate letter sent on Sunday in which the activist investor opposed the appointment of Stephen Dacus as CEO and called for Seven & i to engage with Alimentation Couche-Tard , which is seeking to acquire it.

Foreign investors including Artisan have for some years pushed the company to focus on its core convenience store business.

data.title: US Futures Rise as Tech Stocks Extend Gains
data.description:
US stock futures climbed on Thursday, building on the previous session’s rally led by technology stocks.

In after-hours trading, Intel surged over 10% following the appointment of Lip-Bu Tan as its new CEO, while Adobe slipped 4% after issuing a weaker-than-expected earnings forecast for its fiscal second quarter.

During Wednesday’s regular session, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite gained 0.49% and 1.22%, respectively, snapping a two-day losing streak.

However, the Dow edged down 0.2%, marking its third consecutive decline.

Tech stocks spearheaded the rebound, with notable gains from Nvidia (+6.4%), Tesla (+7.6%), Meta Platforms (+2.3%), Palantir Technologies (+7.2%), and Netflix (+2.8%).

The rally followed a softer-than-expected February inflation report, which helped ease concerns about the economy and the impact of tariffs on prices.

Meanwhile, Trump’s steel and aluminum tariffs took effect, prompting Canada to retaliate with 25% duties on over $20 billion worth of US goods.

data.title: RLF AgTech Signs Distribution Agreement with Delta Agribusiness
data.description:
RLF AgTech signed a distribution agreement with Delta Agribusiness, effective immediately, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

RLF will provide products at wholesale prices, offer rebates, and financially contribute to marketing campaigns for Delta Ag, the filing said.

The parties may terminate the agreement by mutual agreement or by one party giving the other at least 90 days' notice.
